verdict_reasoning: HSPA5/GRP78/BiP is canonically an ER-luminal chaperone with no transmembrane domain or membrane anchor. However, it is one of the best-characterized 'cancer ecto-chaperones': tumor cells and stressed cells display GRP78 on the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane, where it acts as a co-receptor, signal transducer, and target of cell-surface-directed therapeutics (antibodies, ADCs, bispecific programs). This surface display is documented in cancer, ER stress, viral infection, and apoptotic cell death contexts — all qualifying as cell-state-induced. The surface pool is real, wash-resistant (not merely a reversible secreted binding event), and clinically exploited. Baseline normal-cell localization is ER-luminal, so this is not `yes`, but the cancer/stress ecto-pool is too well-documented to call `no`. Checking other contextual buckets: not lysosomal-exocytosis-dependent; not a germline-restricted TM protein; no known TM partner stable tethering. The dominant contextual mechanism is cell-state-induced stress/tumor PM translocation.
